opp.name: Mizuho Americas [S2P] 2024
opp.next_step: July 20 - follow up Procurement Strategy Update Meeting Overview Edward Dlugosz, George Andrus, and Lee Teney (the two US decision makers) discussed the status of their procurement initiative at Mizuho, which has been paused due to the need for a global procurement framework. Lee expressed gratitude for the team's work and acknowledged the six-month delay. Edward offered to provide information on Zycus' user group event in Colorado and mentioned a new client in the Middle East. George emphasized the importance of regular updates and mentioned Zycus' impressive technology and team. The team agreed to stay in touch and share updates as they become available. Action Items: Edward Dlugosz - Research Zycus’s penetration into the Japanese market and clients using the platform in native Japanese-based environments, and send a summary report to Lee, George, Matt, and Alex. Edward Dlugosz - Send detailed information about the upcoming Zycus user group event (including dates, location, and agenda) to Lee and George today. Edward Dlugosz - Provide periodic updates to Lee and George on company and product news, including new clients and deployments in Asia, as material developments occur. Edward Dlugosz - Inform Lee and George of the name of the new Middle East client once the publicity clause allows public disclosure. Edward Dlugosz - When significant material updates arise (such as new client wins or major product changes), ping Matt and Alex directly instead of waiting for the next monthly check-in. Review the user group event information that Zycus sends and evaluate whether to attend. Update on Mizuho Initiative Lee Teney informs Edward Dlugosz and George Andrus that their management has asked for a longer pause on the Mizuho initiative. The pause is due to the need for a global framework for procurement and cost management, which varies by region. Lee Teney expresses gratitude for the work done so far and acknowledges the difficulty of the pause. The pause is estimated to last at least six months, and the team is currently treating the project as on hold. Edward Dlugosz confirms that Zycus has clients in Japan and shares an example of a long evaluation process with Panasonic North America. Invitation to User Group Event Edward Dlugosz invites the team to attend a user group event in Colorado, highlighting the benefits of meeting with analysts and other customers. He offers to help with travel restrictions if necessary and promises to send information about the event. The team expresses interest in the event and plans to review the information provided. Edward Dlugosz suggests regular check-ins with Alex and Emmett to stay in touch and share updates. Final Remarks and Next Steps Edward Dlugosz asks if he should check in with Alex and Emmett on a monthly basis, and the team agrees. Lee Teney and George Andrus discuss the importance of updates on company and product news, especially new clients in Asia. Edward Dlugosz shares that Zycus has secured its first client in the Middle East, despite the challenges in the region. The meeting concludes with the team expressing appreciation for the information and plans to stay in touch.
opp.ais_why:
• Tavannie Perez made the explicit AI ask on the 12/9/2025 call: "is there any AI functionality that we haven't spoken about that maybe enhances the process today?" She wanted it for both vendor onboarding and invoicing. Zycus committed to send a documented list of available AI-based functionalities, which is buyer-pulled, not vendor-pushed.
• The demo landed on AP Smartdesk: an AI agent that plugs into the AP inbox, classifies incoming email as invoice, query, or other, and applies fraud and duplicate checks upfront. Mizuho recognized AP Compass anomaly detection and dynamic discounting as a fit for their automated compliance checks, and they probed Zycus's OCR sophistication.
• This is BFSI conservatism in motion. David Wong approval is required for any meaningful process change, 19 prior Avoma calls show the pace is grinding, and most discussion still centers on AP automation rather than agentic procurement. The AI interest is real but tactical, which is why this lands mid-band.

sources: SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (global framework need)
No Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, or Value_to_Customer__c fields populated
metrics.narrative: No quantified business case has been articulated across any available evidence. The buyer's stated driver is the need for a 'global framework for procurement and cost management, which varies by region' (Next_Step__c, Jun 2026), indicating enterprise-wide procurement transformation scope, but no financial metrics, headcount savings, cycle-time reduction, error-rate improvement, or ROI targets have been documented in Salesforce fields or calls. The business problem is framed at a strategic governance level (global consistency) rather than operational pain with measurable impact. Without quantified value, executive sponsorship and budget approval remain abstract. The highest-leverage move is to secure a discovery session when the pause lifts that quantifies regional procurement inefficiency, manual workload, and cost leakage across Mizuho's global footprint, then tie Zycus deployment to specific savings targets.

sources: SF Contact Roles (no Champion role assigned; George/Lee are Decision Maker/Evaluator, not advocates)
SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (passive monthly check-ins with Alex, Matthew)
No evidence of internal advocacy, intel sharing, or deal acceleration behaviors
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified or developed. George Andrus and Lee Tenny are senior decision-makers but have not demonstrated advocacy, internal selling, or access-brokering behaviors. Their role in the Jun 2026 call was to communicate the pause decision made by higher management, not to push back on it or advocate for Zycus acceleration. Alex Jaffee (Global Strategic Sourcing Professional, Evaluator contact role) and Matthew Brzoza (VP Procurement, Influencer contact role) are the agreed monthly check-in contacts (SF Next_Step__c), indicating ongoing relationship maintenance, but no evidence of them driving the deal forward, sharing internal strategy, or providing competitive intel. Scott Buckleton is mapped as Executive Sponsor, but no recent engagement or sponsorship evidence exists. The deal is stuck in a passive, vendor-managed nurture mode with no internal advocate pushing for re-activation. The highest-priority move is to recruit a champion from the US procurement team (likely Alex or Matthew, given their ongoing engagement) by providing them with value-add content (Japan market penetration data, Merlin AI differentiation, peer references) that positions them as informed advocates when the global framework discussion resumes, and ask them to surface the internal timeline and stakeholders driving the framework decision.

sources: SF Competitors__c (8 competitors listed)
SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (Japan market research request implies regional capability as differentiator)
Stage: Shortlisted (competitive filtering occurred)
competition.narrative: Mizuho is running a competitive evaluation against eight named vendors: Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Sirion Labs, and DocuSign (SF Competitors__c). Coupa is inferred as the primary competitive threat given its enterprise S2P footprint and Asia-Pacific presence. The deal reached Shortlisted stage, meaning Zycus survived initial competitive filtering, but the pause has frozen competitive positioning before deeper differentiation could occur. No recent competitive intelligence, head-to-head demos, or buyer feedback on competitive strengths/weaknesses has been captured. The rep's action item to research Zycus Japan market penetration and native Japanese platform support (SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026) suggests the buyer is evaluating global/regional deployment capability as a key differentiator, and competitors with stronger Japan presence (potentially SAP Ariba or Oracle given their enterprise ERP footprint) may have an edge. The wedge is Merlin AI for procurement intelligence and negotiation automation, positioned as next-gen capability the legacy competitors lack, plus Zycus's proven enterprise S2P deployment track record. The move is to deliver a competitive battle card to the buyer when re-engagement begins, highlighting Zycus's Japan client base, native-language support, and Merlin AI differentiation, and ask the buyer directly how Zycus scored vs. Coupa/Ariba/GEP in their evaluation to surface any competitive gaps early.

sources: SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (strategic framework need, not operational pain)
No Customer_Business_Problem__c or Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c populated
6-month pause tolerance indicates low urgency/pain
identify_pain.narrative: Specific operational or financial pain driving urgency has not been articulated by the buyer. The stated driver is strategic: the need for a 'global framework for procurement and cost management, which varies by region' (SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026). This frames the initiative as governance-led standardization rather than pain-driven urgency. No pain points in current procurement stack, manual process inefficiencies, supplier-management failures, contract leakage, or compliance gaps have been documented in Salesforce fields (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Pain_points_in_the_current_solution__c not populated) or calls. The absence of articulated pain correlates with the lack of quantified metrics and explains the willingness to pause for 6+ months: without acute operational or financial pain, the project is a strategic nice-to-have, not a must-have. The move is to conduct a pain-discovery workshop when re-engagement begins, focusing on regional procurement inefficiency evidence (manual workflows, spend visibility gaps, supplier risk exposure, contract compliance failures) that the global framework is intended to solve, and tie Zycus deployment to measurable pain relief.

sources: No contracting, legal, or InfoSec discussion in available evidence
No Current_Contract_Expiration__c populated
Stage: Shortlisted (pre-commercials)
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review, procurement channel, InfoSec requirements, and MSA/SOW scoping have not been discussed or documented. The deal has not progressed to commercials or negotiation, so paper-process visibility is genuinely absent. No Current_Contract_Expiration__c date is populated, so there is no incumbent-contract forcing function. No SI/partner involvement has been noted in Salesforce or calls. The buyer's stated need for a global framework may introduce centralized contracting requirements, multi-jurisdiction legal review (US + Japan at minimum), and enterprise-wide InfoSec/data-governance standards that are currently undefined. The move is to surface Zycus's enterprise MSA template, multi-region data residency options, and InfoSec certification portfolio (SOC2, ISO, etc.) early in re-engagement to de-risk the paper process before it becomes a bottleneck, and ask the buyer whether global legal/procurement will own contracting or delegate to regional teams.

sources: SF Contact Role: George Andrus, Decision Maker

SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (George and Lee on pause call)
SF Contact Role: Takashi Minowa, Deputy CFO, Influencer
economic_buyer.narrative: George Andrus (Executive Director, Head of Procurement) is mapped as Decision Maker in Contact Roles and has been the senior US procurement authority across all engagement. Lee Tenny (Head of Procurement and Third Party Risk Management) is mapped as Evaluator and co-leads US procurement decisions alongside George. Both participated in the Jun 2026 call where the global pause was communicated (Next_Step__c). Scott Buckleton (VP Procurement) is mapped as both Executive Sponsor and Evaluator, indicating broader oversight. However, the stated pause 'due to management request for a global framework' signals that budget authority for this enterprise procurement transformation may sit above the US procurement leads, likely with global CFO/COO or a Japan-based executive (Takashi Minowa, Deputy CFO, is listed as Influencer contact role). Active EB engagement is absent: no pricing, commercials, or ROI discussions in recent history. The commercial conversation has not advanced beyond initial scoping. The move is to map the global budget holder when the pause lifts and secure their sponsorship before re-engaging the US team on commercials.

sources: SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (management-driven global pause, 6-month estimate)
SF Contact Roles (US leads mapped, no global authority identified)
Stage: Shortlisted (pre-pause progression)
SF Next_Step__c (monthly check-ins with Alex, Matthew)
decision_process.narrative: The decision process shifted from a US-led procurement initiative to a global governance-driven framework requirement, pausing the US evaluation. Initially, the US procurement leads (George Andrus, Lee Tenny) were driving vendor selection and had shortlisted Zycus. The pause announced in Jun 2026 indicates that a higher global authority (referred to as 'management' in Next_Step__c) intervened to mandate a global procurement framework before regional implementations proceed. This suggests a two-tier approval: (1) global framework definition and budget allocation at corporate/Japan HQ level, (2) regional implementation approval by US procurement once framework is set. No formal approval chain, governance committee, or decision timeline has been documented. The buyer is 'treating the project as on hold' with an estimated 6-month minimum pause. Monthly check-ins with Alex Jaffee and Matthew Brzoza (SF Next_Step__c action) indicate a nurture-only engagement model, not an active decision process. The move is to map the global decision-making body and their timeline for framework completion, and secure a commitment to re-enter active evaluation once the framework is approved.

sources: SF Products__c (8 products in scope)
SF Competitors__c (8 competitors)
SF Next_Step__c Jun 2026 (Japanese platform capability research request)
Stage: Shortlisted (implies criteria-based vendor selection occurred)
decision_criteria.narrative: Evaluation criteria have not been formally documented in Salesforce or extracted from structured use-case validation calls. The deal reached Shortlisted stage, implying vendor shortlisting against unstated criteria occurred prior to the pause. Product scope is defined (iContract, iSource, ML iAnalyze, iManage, iSupplier, eProcurement, iSaaS, Merlin Intake per SF Products__c), suggesting functional requirements were mapped during earlier discovery, but the weighted decision criteria, test-case results, and validation outcomes are not on record. Competitors in scope (Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER, Oracle, SAP Ariba, Sirion Labs, DocuSign per SF Competitors__c) indicate a competitive evaluation, but how Zycus scored against these rivals on specific criteria is unknown. The stated need for 'native Japanese-based environment' support (SF Next_Step__c action item for rep to research Zycus Japan penetration) surfaces one inferred criterion: multi-region/language platform capability to support global rollout. The move is to request the formal RFP/RFI scorecard or evaluation matrix from the buyer when re-engagement begins, and confirm whether AI/Merlin capabilities factored into shortlisting.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. It's genuinely competitive and could go either way; the reads below say where it stands.

contributions.evidence: Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found.

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 34 is driven by Stage is ahead of the engagement/evidence the sweep found; Verdict 'Off Track' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 49 is carried by exec access strength +1.00 (weight 15); competitive strength +0.20 (weight 15). Offsetting it: differentiation strength -0.40 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 54 (roughly flat): Next Step carries 1 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 5 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, exec and champion, commercial, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment 55 reflects real customer investment: Customer owns 5 open commitment(s); Direct economic-buyer access recorded.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 33 is anchored on the stage win (49), nudged by commitment 55 and momentum 54, and dragged by risk 34.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Merlin Intake is scoped (SF Products__c), indicating baseline AI interest, but no AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, or AIS_Why__c fields are populated, so buyer AI appetite is unrated by the system. No call evidence of buyer articulating AI needs, Merlin demo attendance, or agentic-procurement enthusiasm. The buyer's request for Japan market penetration research (SF Next_Step__c) and global framework focus suggest operational/regional deployment concerns are foremost, not AI innovation. AI fit is unknown but likely Curious (interested but not leading with AI as a driver). The move is to surface Merlin AI capabilities early in re-engagement as a next-gen differentiator that solves global procurement complexity (multi-language negotiation intelligence, supplier risk analysis, intake automation) and ask the buyer whether AI/ML features factored into their shortlisting decision.

north_star_verdict.math: 80 days to close (Sep 22). In Shortlisted stage since at least Jun 2026 (latest field modification Jun 17). Last activity Jun 22 (12 days ago). Deal created May 24, 2024 (774 days old). No submission-date fields populated to measure true stage velocity. Buyer explicitly paused initiative for at least 6 months as of last call, treating project as on hold. Close date Sep 2026 is unrealistic against a declared pause extending into late 2026 or 2027. No active buyer-driven evaluation activity; rep-initiated monthly check-ins only.

hard.ais_why:
• Tavannie Perez made the explicit AI ask on the 12/9/2025 call: "is there any AI functionality that we haven't spoken about that maybe enhances the process today?" She wanted it for both vendor onboarding and invoicing. Zycus committed to send a documented list of available AI-based functionalities, which is buyer-pulled, not vendor-pushed.
• The demo landed on AP Smartdesk: an AI agent that plugs into the AP inbox, classifies incoming email as invoice, query, or other, and applies fraud and duplicate checks upfront. Mizuho recognized AP Compass anomaly detection and dynamic discounting as a fit for their automated compliance checks, and they probed Zycus's OCR sophistication.
• This is BFSI conservatism in motion. David Wong approval is required for any meaningful process change, 19 prior Avoma calls show the pace is grinding, and most discussion still centers on AP automation rather than agentic procurement. The AI interest is real but tactical, which is why this lands mid-band.
hard.next_step:
July 20 - follow up


Procurement Strategy Update Meeting

Overview

Edward Dlugosz, George Andrus, and Lee Teney (the two US decision makers) discussed the status of their procurement initiative at Mizuho, which has been paused due to the need for a global procurement framework. Lee expressed gratitude for the team's work and acknowledged the six-month delay. Edward offered to provide information on Zycus' user group event in Colorado and mentioned a new client in the Middle East. George emphasized the importance of regular updates and mentioned Zycus' impressive technology and team. The team agreed to stay in touch and share updates as they become available.


Action Items:

  • Edward Dlugosz - Research Zycus’s penetration into the Japanese market and clients using the platform in native Japanese-based environments, and send a summary report to Lee, George, Matt, and Alex.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Send detailed information about the upcoming Zycus user group event (including dates, location, and agenda) to Lee and George today.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Provide periodic updates to Lee and George on company and product news, including new clients and deployments in Asia, as material developments occur.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Inform Lee and George of the name of the new Middle East client once the publicity clause allows public disclosure.
  • Edward Dlugosz - When significant material updates arise (such as new client wins or major product changes), ping Matt and Alex directly instead of waiting for the next monthly check-in.
  • Review the user group event information that Zycus sends and evaluate whether to attend.

Update on Mizuho Initiative

  • Lee Teney informs Edward Dlugosz and George Andrus that their management has asked for a longer pause on the Mizuho initiative.
  • The pause is due to the need for a global framework for procurement and cost management, which varies by region.
  • Lee Teney expresses gratitude for the work done so far and acknowledges the difficulty of the pause.
  • The pause is estimated to last at least six months, and the team is currently treating the project as on hold.
  • Edward Dlugosz confirms that Zycus has clients in Japan and shares an example of a long evaluation process with Panasonic North America.

Invitation to User Group Event

  • Edward Dlugosz invites the team to attend a user group event in Colorado, highlighting the benefits of meeting with analysts and other customers.
  • He offers to help with travel restrictions if necessary and promises to send information about the event.
  • The team expresses interest in the event and plans to review the information provided.
  • Edward Dlugosz suggests regular check-ins with Alex and Emmett to stay in touch and share updates.

Final Remarks and Next Steps

  • Edward Dlugosz asks if he should check in with Alex and Emmett on a monthly basis, and the team agrees.
  • Lee Teney and George Andrus discuss the importance of updates on company and product news, especially new clients in Asia.
  • Edward Dlugosz shares that Zycus has secured its first client in the Middle East, despite the challenges in the region.
  • The meeting concludes with the team expressing appreciation for the information and plans to stay in touch.

July 20 - follow up


Procurement Strategy Update Meeting

Overview

Edward Dlugosz, George Andrus, and Lee Teney (the two US decision makers) discussed the status of their procurement initiative at Mizuho, which has been paused due to the need for a global procurement framework. Lee expressed gratitude for the team's work and acknowledged the six-month delay. Edward offered to provide information on Zycus' user group event in Colorado and mentioned a new client in the Middle East. George emphasized the importance of regular updates and mentioned Zycus' impressive technology and team. The team agreed to stay in touch and share updates as they become available.


Action Items:

  • Edward Dlugosz - Research Zycus’s penetration into the Japanese market and clients using the platform in native Japanese-based environments, and send a summary report to Lee, George, Matt, and Alex.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Send detailed information about the upcoming Zycus user group event (including dates, location, and agenda) to Lee and George today.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Provide periodic updates to Lee and George on company and product news, including new clients and deployments in Asia, as material developments occur.
  • Edward Dlugosz - Inform Lee and George of the name of the new Middle East client once the publicity clause allows public disclosure.
  • Edward Dlugosz - When significant material updates arise (such as new client wins or major product changes), ping Matt and Alex directly instead of waiting for the next monthly check-in.
  • Review the user group event information that Zycus sends and evaluate whether to attend.

Update on Mizuho Initiative

  • Lee Teney informs Edward Dlugosz and George Andrus that their management has asked for a longer pause on the Mizuho initiative.
  • The pause is due to the need for a global framework for procurement and cost management, which varies by region.
  • Lee Teney expresses gratitude for the work done so far and acknowledges the difficulty of the pause.
  • The pause is estimated to last at least six months, and the team is currently treating the project as on hold.
  • Edward Dlugosz confirms that Zycus has clients in Japan and shares an example of a long evaluation process with Panasonic North America.

Invitation to User Group Event

  • Edward Dlugosz invites the team to attend a user group event in Colorado, highlighting the benefits of meeting with analysts and other customers.
  • He offers to help with travel restrictions if necessary and promises to send information about the event.
  • The team expresses interest in the event and plans to review the information provided.
  • Edward Dlugosz suggests regular check-ins with Alex and Emmett to stay in touch and share updates.

Final Remarks and Next Steps

  • Edward Dlugosz asks if he should check in with Alex and Emmett on a monthly basis, and the team agrees.
  • Lee Teney and George Andrus discuss the importance of updates on company and product news, especially new clients in Asia.
  • Edward Dlugosz shares that Zycus has secured its first client in the Middle East, despite the challenges in the region.
  • The meeting concludes with the team expressing appreciation for the information and plans to stay in touch.
